title: Competing Effect of Biquadratic and Heisenberg Coupling on Magnetic Tunnel Junction Molecular Spintronics Devices
authors: Andoniaina Mariah Randriambololona, Hayden Brown, Eva Mutunga, Andrew Grizzle, Christopher DAngelo, Pawan Tyagi
abstract: Heisenberg exchange coupling (HC) and biquadratic exchange coupling (BQC) are known to occur in magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJ) and nanoscale spintronics structures. MTJ-based molecular spintronics devices (MTJMSD) provide a platform to study these interactions and the correlated magnetic behavior they generate. Molecular spin channels formed along the exposed MTJ edge have been shown to produce strong exchange interactions that include HC and BQC, which can drive perpendicular alignment of spin vectors in adjacent ferromagnetic electrodes. Despite their importance, the competing roles of HC and BQC in MTJMSDs remain unclear. Monte Carlo simulations using a three-dimensional Heisenberg model were performed to systematically vary BQC strength under three conditions: no molecular HC, strong parallel HC, and strong antiparallel HC. The resulting magnetic and physical properties of the MTJMSDs were analyzed. Increasing BQC strength produced minimal changes in overall magnetization when strong HC was present, indicating that HC dominates device magnetization. Temporal evolution studies showed that devices with only BQC failed to reach magnetic stability, while devices with both HC and BQC achieved stable magnetic states due to the stabilizing influence of HC. These results show that BQC plays a secondary role in magnetization dynamics and cannot overcome the stronger stabilizing effect of HC. The presence of BQC offers a plausible explanation for experimentally observed magnetic phase orientations beyond simple parallel and antiparallel states in MTJMSDs.
summary: This paper explores how Heisenberg and biquadratic exchange couplings compete to determine the magnetic state of molecular spintronic devices. It finds that while Heisenberg coupling dictates the primary magnetic alignment, biquadratic coupling helps stabilize the system against thermal fluctuations. These findings help explain experimental observations of non-standard magnetic phase orientations.
main_problem: Investigating the competing effects of Heisenberg exchange coupling (HC) and biquadratic exchange coupling (BQC) on the magnetic stability and spin orientation in magnetic tunnel junction molecular spintronics devices (MTJMSD).
main_result: Heisenberg coupling is the dominant force determining parallel or antiparallel magnetization, while BQC plays a secondary role by assisting in magnetic stabilization and reducing susceptibility to thermal fluctuations.
method: Monte Carlo simulations using the Metropolis algorithm and a 3D Heisenberg/Ising model to study temporal evolution and equilibrium states.
model_or_system: A Magnetic Tunnel Junction Molecular Spintronics Device (MTJMSD) consisting of a ferromagnet-insulator-ferromagnet (FM-I-FM) trilayer with paramagnetic molecules at the exposed edges, modeled with an expanded Hamiltonian including HC and BQC terms.
key_observables: Device magnetization, spin orientation angles, temporal evolution of magnetization, and 2-D autocorrelation of molecular and electrode spins.
important_parameters: Heisenberg coupling strength (J), biquadratic coupling strength (b), thermal energy (kT), and device geometry (pillar vs. extended cross junction).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the BQC is a localized phenomenon at the junction and uses a simplified 3D model for the electrodes.
figures_summary: Schematics of the MTJMSD and molecular interactions; autocorrelation graphs; temporal evolution of magnetization; and contour plots of spin angles as a function of coupling and temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MTJMSD problem, describes the Hamiltonian and Monte Carlo methodology, presents results for varying BQC under different HC regimes, analyzes thermal effects, and concludes with implications for device stability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG 1. (A) MTJMSD with left FM electrode[4] (FM1) and right FM electrode (FM2) with molecules attached to the exposed edges and (B) 3-D molecular representation of the different exchange coupling that exist within the simulations of the device.

FIG 2. Lattice plots of (A) MTJMSD with extended cross junction and (B) MTJMSD pillar structure. Both configurations are comprised of a FM-molecules-FM structure with 5x5 molecules around the edges. In these specific simulations, both the top and bottom edges are turned off.

FIG 3. Condition 1 temporal evolution graphs for (a)no (BmL/BmR 0/0) (b) moderate (BmL/BmR 0.5/0.5) and (c) strong (BmL/BmR 1/1) BQC in an MTJMSD with extended cross junctions and (a)no, (b)moderate and (c) strong BQC coupling in an MTJMSD with pillar MTJMSD, respectively. For all simulations under this condition, no HC exists in the molecules.

FIG 4. 2-D Autocorrelation graphs for (A) no (B) moderate and (C) strong BQC given no molecular HC is introduced to the system. The autocorrelation charts visually depict how correlated the molecules are with respect to the left and right electrodes.

FIG 5. Temporal evolutions graphs for condition 2 for the extended cross junction MTJMSD under (A) no, (B) moderate, and (C) strong BQC and the pillar under (D), (E) moderate and (F) strong BQC. Both extended cross junction and pillar MTJMSD simulations under condition 2 exhibit FM behavior.
